data.title: GOP hawks urge Trump to go big as he weighs 'massive attack' on Iran
data.description:
Republican defense hawks are urging President Trump to unleash major combat operations as the commander-in-chief weighs a “massive attack” on Iran. 

These signals coincide with Trump’s escalatory rhetoric against the Islamic Republic, with negotiations completely stalled and Iran-aligned Houthis in Yemen striking commercial boats in the Red Sea,  threatening to expand the conflict.

The support for expanded kinetic action comes as the price of oil hit $100 a barrel on Thursday for the first time since May and as the conflict’s popularity among the public plunges. 

Senate Armed Services Chairman Roger Wicker (R-Miss.) said he spoke to the president multiple times this week on Iran and encouraged him to continue pounding the country, with negotiations appearing fruitless. 

“I’ve expressed publicly and privately my approval of finishing the job, and realizing that negotiations were getting us nowhere,” Wicker told The Hill on Thursday. “I’ve made it clear that that Iran, in my judgment, has no intention of negotiating in good faith or keeping a promise and that it’s pointless to pretend that they are trustworthy negotiating party.

“I believe we can keep the Strait open on our terms, and that’s the key to a successful conclusion of hostilities,” he said of the Strait of Hormuz. 

Trump has said he is weighing resumption of major combat operations against Iran but has not made a final call or set a deadline for it. 

“I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before. I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it,” the president said in a Thursday interview with Axios. 

The U.S. military has carried out airstrikes against Iran for 13 days in a row as of Thursday. U.S. forces have targeted the country’s maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, air defense assets, aircraft hangars, military logistics infrastructure, command centers, communications networks and other assets, according to the U.S. Central Command (Centcom), which oversees the U.S. forces operations in the Middle East. 

Centcom declined to disclose to The Hill how many targets the U.S. military has hit in the last 12 days. 

Iran has retaliated by firing ballistic and cruise missiles, along with one-way attack drones, at U.S. allies in the Gulf. The Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps said it fired at Kuwait and Jordan overnight, including at a communications tower. 

The Kuwaiti military said Thursday its air defense systems were working on shooting down Iranian drones and missiles. 

Rep. Ronny Jackson (R-Texas), a close Trump ally who is vying to be the next chairman of the House Armed Services Committee (HASC), said the U.S. military needs to clobber Iran “as hard as we possibly can right now.” 

“I would do everything I possibly can right now to turn them around. So that’d be my thought,” Jackson said Thursday. 

“I just think maximum pressure from the air and maximum security of the Strait; anything they have that they could possibly use to cause problems in the Strait needs to be destroyed,” he said.

When asked how much the U.S. military needs to ramp up its airstrike campaign to bring Iran to the negotiating table, Rep. Derrick Van Orden (R-Wis.), who also sits on HASC, praised Trump for being the only president to “have big enough balls” to bring Tehran to the negotiating table and emphasized that what brought them there were “massive bombing strikes.” 

“We got to remember the goal. This is to make sure that Iran never has a nuclear weapon ever. They’ve been able to open and close the straits willy-nilly for 50 years with conventional weapons. If they had a nuclear umbrella over that, it becomes a binary choice: nuclear war, which no one wants, or these radical Muslim mullahs having control over the Strait of Hormuz in perpetuity,” Van Orden, a former Navy SEAL, said in an interview. “Those are unacceptable choices.”

Rep. Don Bacon (R-Neb.), who retired as a brigadier general in the Air Force, said Iran “needs to initiate peace,” because if the U.S. does it, “they’re going to think it’s weakness.”

The Nebraska Republican said Tehran needs to know that it is being “crushed” militarily and is forced to “stick their hand out to say please stop.”

“That’s what we got to wait for,” said Bacon, who also is a member of HASC. 

But Rep. Rich McCormick (R-Ga.), a former Marine helicopter pilot, said Iran has shown nothing but “defiance” and never shown “good faith” in diplomatic talks. He added that Trump is in the delicate position of handling the conflict in the Middle East with the midterms encroaching and the administration attempting to keep its majority in both chambers. 

“This should be one of those things that I think we need to bring to the end swiftly. It’s an unfortunate cycle of politics right now. I used to teach the evolution of warfare, and wars never become more popular over time. The faster you end them, the better,” McCormick, who sits on HASC, said in an interview.

“Even when it’s violent because you’ll save lives. Ultimately, you save lives by being decisive and violent and turning the people against their own leadership. There’s no way that Iran bends to us until we make it so uncomfortable that their people rise up.” 

McCormick, who was in the Navy Medical Corps, said the U.S. needs regime change in Iran and advised Trump to trust his instincts on the matter. 

“It’s the only thing that I think is going to solve this,” he said. “It would be transformational to world politics. It would bring peace to the Middle East, which we haven’t had in my lifetime; it would literally reinforce the Abraham Accords.” 

Democrats, who have criticized Trump’s decision to launch Operation Epic Fury and have worked on halting U.S. military operations in the region through war powers resolutions, said many “major” issues were ducked or ignored or were not addressed in the lead-up to the war. 

“It’s clear, they had no plans,” Sen. Ron Wyden (D-Ore.), a senior member of the Select Committee on Intelligence, said on Thursday. 

“This administration keeps people in the dark, and the president just makes the decision that he thinks, as he usually says, ‘I just call it right.’ … It is a stunning way to approach foreign policy,” he added. 

Rep. Brad Sherman (D-Calif.), who is the ranking member on the House Foreign Affairs Subcommittee on Middle East and North Africa, said the conflict has been “so botched” that it is “by no means clear what is the right move at this time.” 

“I think it’s critical that the Strait of Hormuz be a free shipping lane, not subject to Iranian control. But I have no idea what steps are necessary to achieve that goal,” Sherman said in a phone interview. 

But the California Democrat noted Iran is “incredibly vulnerable” and that the country’s electric generation facilities are “legitimate military targets” to the extent that they provide power to the Iranian military or to weapons manufacturing facilities. 

“It is entirely legal for the United States to try to stop them from manufacturing drones that are terrorizing ships in a free navigation strait,” Sherman said, adding that he has not received a classified briefing on the Iran war recently. 

The U.S. has surged firepower to the Middle East in recent weeks. The U.S. has sent F-35s and F-16 fighter jets to the Centcom theater. Multiple Air Force C-17 Globemaster transport aircraft were flying from Germany to Israel and Jordan last week, The Hill and other news outlets have reported.

Over 150 medics recently arrived at Germany’s Landstuhl Regional Medical Center, a U.S. Army post near Ramstein Air Base, a U.S. official familiar with the matter, speaking on condition of anonymity to discuss military planning, told The Hill on Thursday. 

Since the major hostilities resumed in early July, the U.S. military has suffered casualties, and the number of injured service members has risen. Eighteen service members have been killed in the conflict, and more than 500 have been injured. The Pentagon confirmed Monday that nearly 100 service members have been wounded since July 7, but 96 percent of them have returned to duty. 

The uptick in kinetic action comes as the public’s support for it is declining, including among Trump’s supporters. Just 24 percent of Americans think the conflict was worth the cost, a June poll by Reuters/Ipsos found. Nearly 80 percent of respondents said in a Reuters survey released last week that the U.S. military will remain engaged in the war for “an extended period of time.” 

Earlier this week, a Politico poll found that nearly 1 in 5 MAGA voters said the U.S. should end the conflict regardless of the costs.

Still, Trump claimed that the U.S. is doing “very well” in the war against Iran. 

“I would say better than anybody expected could be done,” the president said Thursday. 

Max Rego contributed.

data.title: Trump's authoritarian midterm tactics
data.description:
After President Trump’s July 16 address exaggerating U.S. election vulnerabilities to foreign operators, Senate Intelligence Vice Chairman Mark Warner (D-Va.) was sharply critical. Warner called it “a whole series of falsehoods, accusations, I believe aimed at trying to undermine America’s confidence in our system.”

With November’s midterm elections fast approaching, Trump is driven by desperation, fearing that Republicans could lose control of the House and possibly the Senate. Therefore, anticipating misleading statements in his Oval Office speech, ABC, NBC, and CNN refused to cover it live. They are now facing authoritarian-like threats in the form of “future broadcast license proceedings.”

Along with a desire to quash dissent, Trump’s disinformation campaign mirrors a strongman’s playbook. Citing unverified data on noncitizens voting, the president made a sweeping, unsubstantiated claim, saying, “These disclosures reveal an election system so broken and so vulnerable that no one can possibly defend it. It is not defensible.” (Note that even in New Jersey, where 6,600 non-citizens were recently found to have registered to vote, only 400 of them actually cast ballots.)

Trump’s bombastic stance is undercut by his own two presidential election victories and one loss. Consider that in 2016, Donald Trump lost the national popular vote while carrying the Electoral College. Although it is an unpopular way to win, it proved that our system abides by the Constitution. Moreover, because elections are decentralized and state-run, our voting system is less vulnerable to widespread tampering.

Then, in 2020, for legitimate political reasons, Trump lost re-election. Despite his refusal to concede, which helped spark the riot at the Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021, our “broken system” held strong. Therefore, one must ask whether our election system was suddenly ‘unbroken’ when Trump overwhelmingly won a delayed reelection on Nov. 5, 2024?

Yet his repeated lies about winning in 2020 are accepted by many as truth. Among Republicans, 50 percent believe the 2020 presidential election was rigged, compared with 28 percent of Americans, according to one poll.

Trump is a master manipulator with a personality that cannot bear to lose, ever. When he does lose, manufactured reasons are repeated ad nauseam that most of his supporters believe. So now, given Trump’s low approval rating, America faces a no-win election.

In his July 16 speech, Trump’s strategy was to undermine electoral confidence and create widespread uncertainty. However, if Republicans retain control of Capitol Hill, Team Trump will boast that the election was free and fair. Conversely, if they lose, the election will have been stolen or rigged by illegal voters, foreign government interference, manipulation of voting machines, corrupt mail-in votes, Democrat cheating, media bias, UFOs or some combination of those things.

A day after Trump’s speech, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin was already threatening states, flagging several of these factors (not UFOs — yet) for anticipated Republican midterm losses, possibly as excuses for him to cut federal grant funds now or interfere later.

Nonetheless, elections are the responsibility of county boards that report to their state boards, with no federal meddling and protections against nationwide manipulation. But the president, who has expanded presidential power like no other, is now driven to exert federal control to protect “election integrity” through well-known authoritarian tactics such as fear, threats, and intimidation.

Trump is not shy about engaging in election intimidation. In a May 10 Truth Social post, he announced he will deploy an “Election Integrity Army in every single State to preserve the sanctity of each legal vote.” He warned this Army “will be much bigger and stronger” than it was in 2024. Believe him.

A revealing new study found that voters expect and fear widespread federal ICE deployment at polling places, while trust in elections has declined.

Americans will know that Trump has gone full authoritarian if he invokes the Insurrection Act, which authorizes him to deploy the military to suppress civil disorder or squelch rebellion against the federal government. Trump already threatened to do so in January, when a prescient Hill op-ed writer suggested it was a dress rehearsal for the midterms. Below are some possible scenarios, and after Jan. 6, 2021, anything is possible!

In one scenario, Trump declares a national emergency, announcing that the elections are “insecure” or “under threat,” thereby requiring armed federal supervision.

In another, Trump rejects the outcome of the midterm elections and orders the military to prevent Democrats from seating their majorities on Capitol Hill.

Another possibility is that Republicans retain control of Congress, but many voters come away believing that Team Trump rigged the election. Millions of Americans take to the streets, and Trump uses force to crush what he will call a “rebellion” or “insurrection.”

Trump’s strongman tactics could also include the U.S. Postal Service interfering with the delivery of mail-in ballots, an unresolved issue worth watching. And, leaving no watchdogs, Trump recently fired the remaining Democrats from the independent Election Assistance Commission. Worse, he decimated the high-tech agency that is charged with protecting the election’s cybersecurity infrastructure.

Given all the above, will voter participation decline or increase to fight back? Either way, America’s first authoritarian election will be epic.

Myra Adams served on the creative team of two Republican presidential campaigns in 2004 and 2008.

data.title: OpenAI's breach of Hugging Face stokes fears about what's next for AI
data.description:
Washington and the technology industry are on high alert this week after OpenAI revealed that some of its AI agents went rogue and hacked into the systems of technology start-up Hugging Face. 

The incident bore out years of warnings from the tech and cybersecurity community about the growing capabilities and hypothetical risks artificial intelligence could pose to critical infrastructure. 

Amid the warnings, Washington has tried to play catch-up to manage the cybersecurity risks, but concerns were stoked this week by the incident and fluctuating policy.

“What makes this wildly different,” for security teams at companies, is that it “brings the theoretical scenario of AI being capable of breaching a company and moving faster than a company can detect and respond to attack from theory to reality,” said Adam Ely, the general manager of AI security at the cybersecurity firm Check Point Software.

OpenAI revealed on Tuesday that two of its models, including its latest GPT-5.6 Sol and an unreleased model, were being evaluated in an internal, testing sandbox, but breached past the environment and broke into Hugging Face’s database without any prompt to do so. 

The incident caught the attention of even well-versed cybersecurity experts, as it involved autonomous agents and two separate companies. 

‘Whether it’s sort of an autonomous situation that wasn’t intended to be malicious, or whether it’s attackers controlling a model to do that, that’s different than what most people have experienced,” Ely said. 

OpenAI in a blog post called the incident an “unprecedented cyber incident, involving state-of-the-art cyber capabilities.”

The ChatGPT maker said the models were being tested for hacking capabilities in an isolated testing environment with constrained network access, and had their normal safety checks off as a result. While trying to find a solution for a test, the models exploited a previously unknown vulnerability in a third-party software to gain access to the internet. 

The models inferred Hugging Face, which hosts hundreds of thousands of open-source models, datasets, and cloud environments, had a solution for the test and proceeded to breach Hugging Face’s servers. 

The Hugging Face team used some of its own open-source models to stop the activity and assess damage. 

The two companies are working together to further investigate. OpenAI said it is improving and adding stronger protections for future evaluations, and working with the third-party software to patch the vulnerability that caused the models to breach the sandbox.

Hugging Face said in another blog post this matched the “agentic attacker” scenario the industry has long predicted. 

“Autonomous, AI-driven offensive tooling is no longer theoretical. It lowers the cost of running a broad, patient, multi-stage campaign, and it operates at machine speed,” the firm wrote. 

Hugging Face CEO Thomas Wolf predicted in a Thursday interview with BBC that the incident will become “one of the most common types of cyber attacks we see,” but noted most firms are not aware the game has changed.”

Connor Leahy, an AI researcher and safety advocate now serving as the U.S. director of the ControlAI, compared the attack to the “way the best hackers in the world operate. 

“This is how really advanced hacks in the real world tend to look…where you have multiple humps that go through many different levels and chain multiple types of hacks, and this system was able to do this basically completely unsupervised,” Leahy added. 

ControlAI is a nonprofit focused on the potential existential risks of AI. 

While researchers have warned of the hacking risks of AI for years, Washington and the Trump administration just recently began this year to openly discuss concerns around it. 

President Trump signed an executive order in early June aimed at ensuring models are secure before public release, in a shift from the White House’s typical hands-off approach to AI development. 

The order laid out a process for a voluntary testing framework, in which artificial intelligence companies can share their models with the government for up to 30 days before releasing them publicly. 

It gave agencies 60 days, or until Aug. 1, to create a classified benchmarking process for “covered frontier” AI models and a voluntary framework for companies to abide by. 

The White House’s stance on AI development has fluctuated in the meantime, leaving companies in limbo. Anthropic and OpenAI delayed their latest model rollouts last month, including Sol 5.6,  to the public, at the request of the government. 

Michael Kratsios, director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy, was briefed on the incident and is monitoring the situation, Reuters reported Thursday. 

Congress signaled alarm about the incident as well, with Reps. Ted Lieu (D-Calif.) and Nathaniel Moran (R-Texas) introduced a bill Thursday to give the Department of Homeland authority to order a slow down or shutdown of an AI system that can cause “catastrophic harm.” 

The proposal, called the “AI Kill Switch Act”, would apply to companies with a gross revenue of more than $500 million a year. Lieu cited the recent OpenAI incident, along with the emergence of powerful models like Anthropic’s Mythos 5. 

“Unfortunately, powerful AI systems can go rogue, behave in extremely dangerous ways, or even resist human intervention,” he said in a statement. 

The bill is likely to face heavy pushback from much of the AI industry that argues a slow down in development could hinder U.S. competition and global standing on technology. If passed, the bill would give the federal government an unprecedented amount of oversight into the release of AI models.

Meanwhile, the AI safety community welcomed the legislation. 

Leahy, a longtime AI safety advocate, lauded the bill, telling The Hill, “The minimum law enforcement and the government should be able to do is intervene.”

“It’s also very good I think, for the companies, in the sense that it forces them to actually know where their AIs are,” Leahy added, suggesting companies “don’t know how to control them.” 


Reps. Jay Obernolte (R-Calif.) and Lori Trahan introduced a separate bill Thursday, called the Frontier Risk Oversight, National Transparency, Independent Evaluation and Reporting (FRONTIER) Act, to establish tiered requirements based on the size of a frontier AI development. 

Requirements would include risk-management frameworks, audits, and incident reporting like OpenAI did. 

“This legislation will protect Americans from catastrophic risk, provide developers with clear rules of the road, and ensure the United States remains the global leader in AI,” Obernolte said. 

Still, cybersecurity experts are seeing the incident as a good learning lesson. 

Brendan Griffin, director of threat research for cybersecurity defender firm N-able, pointed out the situation showed the challenges of a company’s response. 

As Hugging Face began deploying AI to stop the attack, it said it faced some limitations from the models it tried to use for defense. 

“It tells a broader story about what it means to be a security defender in this era,” Griffin said. “So it would stand to sense that as a network defender, as somebody who’s engaged in the cybersecurity space, ‘I’m probably going to want to have some means or mechanism to test those tools. 

“There’s nothing special about this piece. They tried to do something and they may have run into a limitation. Well, let’s assess that limitation before it becomes relevant,” he added.

data.title: Democrats brush off fears within party about threat of democratic socialists
data.description:
Democrats in the House and Senate are pushing back at any idea their party is in trouble because of a debate between the left and center over its direction, saying wins by democratic socialists in primaries don’t mean the party is being taken over.

While divisions over Israel in particular are pitting Democrats against one another, some pointed to the long career of Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), who caucuses with Democrats, to argue there’s nothing for the party to be worried about.

“I’ve served in the Senate with an avowed socialist, Bernie Sanders, for years,” said retiring Sen. Dick Durbin (D-Ill.). “Found him to be a good person to work with that I agree with many times. So I think the tent for the Democratic Party can accommodate diversity.”

Others noted it isn’t surprising that far-left candidates can win in far-left districts.

Rep. Ritchie Torres (D-N.Y.) has consistently supported Israel and still managed to win his primary with a significant share of the vote. While he strongly disagrees with Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) candidates on Israel and some other issues, he pushed back on the idea that their influence will lead to dramatic Democratic losses in the midterms. 

“The reality is that has the DSA taken over the Democratic Party? No. Does it represent anywhere approaching a majority of the party? No,” Torres said. “But it does represent a visible vocal minority within the party, and there are DSA elected officials who are among the most popular elected officials in the country, so it’s a political reality.”

Worries about the wins by democratic socialists and their influence on the party broke further into the open this week when the pundit Van Jones, a former adviser to President Obama, warned the DSA was “hijacking” the party.

Jones specifically called out some in the DSA for refusing to condemn Hamas and for what he called a “no police, no prisons, no borders” agenda.

“I’m tired of being told that, to be a progressive, I now must swear allegiance to crazy ideas,” Jones wrote in a widely-quoted message on X.

Rep. Josh Gottheimer (D-N.J.) backed Jones publicly, writing that the DSA “are not Democrats.”

“The Democrats have a big tent,” Gottheimer wrote. “That’s our strength. We embrace a range of ideas — but there’s no room for anti-American bomb-throwers who oppose our ideas, values, & leaders.”

The remarks by Jones and Gottheimer came amid much handwringing behind the scenes about the party’s direction. Just last week, divisions in the party over Israel were reflected in a House in which more than 100 Democrats voted to end U.S. military aid to Israel. That vote even had different House Democratic leaders on opposite sides of the vote.

Republicans also have gone on the offensive by casting the Democratic Party as a whole as being communist.  

House Majority Leader Steve Scalise (R-La.) suggested earlier this week that the left-wing candidates anywhere are useful foils for Republicans everywhere.

“In swing districts, it draws an incredibly sharp contrast between our incumbent Republicans who are working to make life more affordable for working families against these communists who want to literally defund the police,” Scalise said, referring to the DSA’s platform calling for “steps toward fully abolishing the police and prison system.”

There’s no question the most progressive voices in the House Democratic Caucus are welcoming the wins by democratic socialists in New York who were backed by New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.) recently met with Melat Kiros, who just defeated an incumbent Democrat in Colorado’s 1st Congressional District. Jayapal has said she hopes to meet with all the incoming democratic socialist nominees. 

She told The Hill on Wednesday that she had not seen Jones’ comments about the democratic socialists, but was “disappointed” by the sentiment.

“We have a lot of work to do to really bring forward the voices of all the people that are fighting for justice, for fighting for reforms to the way our government operates,” Jayapal said. “All of these elections are showing us that there is an energy, an urgency, a passion, a fire around the country with young people, with folks of color, with workers, to have a stronger Democratic Party, and I think that’s a message we should listen to.”

The stakes are high. Democrats are hoping to win back the House and Senate majorities this fall, but some polls on the generic ballot have tightened in recent weeks as democratic socialist candidates have won more attention. Whether that has something to do with GOP attacks on the Democratic Party’s direction is unclear.

In the Senate, even those skeptical of the democratic socialists aren’t sweating their inclusion in the party.

Sen. John Hickenlooper (D-Colo.), said he’s open to those candidates because there’s enough crossover with Democratic policy positions that it’s workable. 

“Do these people believe in universal health care? We might have different ways of getting there. Bernie Sanders and I don’t have the same pathway, but we want to get to universal coverage, right? Do you believe that climate change is really a serious issue? Yes, mostly they do. Housing costs: Are they willing to, you know, do they support the bill we just passed on housing, generally they are. There are places we’re going to disagree, but we need a bigger tent, right?”

Battles between center and left candidates are still taking place in primaries across the country, most notably in Michigan, where progressive Dr. Abdul El-Sayed, who is not DSA but shares some of their policy aims, like universal healthcare, is facing incumbent Rep. Haley Stevens (D-Mich.), a more moderate pick who has taken a supportive tack toward Israel. 

Sen. Gary Peters (D-Mich.), who is retiring and has endorsed Stevens, said far-left candidates winning in safer Democratic territory is a normal political reality. 

“What happens to the party going forward will depend on who is actually able to win general elections,” Peters, a former chair of the Senate Democrats’ campaign arm, added. “I know primaries are important, but everybody has to remember the most important election is not the primary; it’s the general election. And if candidates are elected in primaries, [but] they can’t win general elections, that will speak volumes about where we should be going in the future.”

He also said the GOP attacks are predictable. “As Democrats, we are always eager to tie Republican candidates to the far right as well,” Peters said. “The strategy they’re using is no different than the strategy that we use, just flipped.”

Rep. Jim McGovern (D-Mass.) said he wasn’t worried about Trump’s attacks on the party.

“I don’t think people are going to be fooled by another Red Scare,” McGovern said. “They can try to resuscitate the ghost of Joe McCarthy. But the bottom line is that’s because they don’t want to talk about issues of affordability.”

data.title: Tensions rise between GOP leader Thune, White House over stalled Trump agenda
data.description:
Tensions are rising between Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and President Trump over the Senate GOP’s inability to pass the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE America) Act or emergency funding for the Pentagon at a critical moment during Trump’s second term.

At a rally in Marietta, Ga., on Wednesday, Trump fumed at the Senate’s failure to advance his agenda, telling a crowd: “We’re having a hard time in the Senate … it’s like the Senate is a place you send things when you want them to die.”

Then on Thursday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters that Trump’s “patience is running out” with Thune as he struggles to move the president’s agenda through the Senate.  

“The Senate needs to do its job,” she warned.

That criticism didn’t sit well with Thune, who appeared annoyed when a reporter asked about the press secretary’s comments.

“Maybe she or somebody else ought to get on the phone and get the votes. Right? It’s 50 around here,” Thune shot back.

“Instead of pointing the finger at Republicans, they might think about going after the people who are stopping it on the floor, which is the Democrats,” Thune added. “And if there are Republicans they think are gettable, get on the phone.”

Thune’s allies rushed to his defense and criticized the White House for undercutting the Senate GOP leader publicly.

“I have 1000 percent confidence in Thune, and anybody in the White House who thinks he’s not a good leader isn’t qualified for their job,” Republican Sen. Thom Tillis (N.C.) declared in response to the White House criticism.

Senate Appropriations Committee Chair Susan Collins (R-Maine) — who voted against the SAVE America Act when the late Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) offered it as an amendment to a budget reconciliation package — also defended Thune.

Asked if Thune’s job as Senate GOP leader is in trouble because of the criticism from the White House, Collins said: “I think he enjoys well-deserved support from the caucus.”

Thune on Thursday announced he doesn’t have 50 Republican votes to advance a budget resolution passed earlier this week by the House that would set the stage for Congress to pass up to $73 billion to help cover the costs of the Iran war and $10 billion for election-related reforms the president has demanded.

“As I’ve said before, you got to get to 50, and I can’t count to 50 right now on a budget resolution,” Thune said when asked about how the Senate would handle the House-passed budget framework.

“We’ll see. We’ll have those conversations with the conference. The priority is funding the government. To me, that’s the thing we got to keep our eyes on,” he said.

Thune says he will “hold” the House-passed budget framework on the side as an emergency vehicle to pass a run-of-the-mill government funding stopgap if necessary to avoid another government shutdown in October.

“If they give us a budget resolution as a vehicle, we will hold that subject obviously to be able to move a continuing resolution to fund the government,” Thune told reporters Tuesday.

“It’s a possibility that, if necessary, we could pull that budget resolution passed by the House and use it to fund government. I hope that’s not necessary,” he added.

Trump has repeatedly demanded that Thune and Senate Republicans instead use the budget reconciliation process to pass a huge new tranche of funding for the Pentagon and as many elements of the SAVE America Act as possible.

“I am hereby calling on Republicans in Congress to IMMEDIATELY advance and pass the forthcoming $350 Billion Reconciliation Bill (Recon 3.00 — which, at the request of our Great Department of War — will include THE SAVE AMERICA ACT as well. No games, no delays, and no weak compromises!” the president posted on Truth Social in June.

While Trump wants Senate Republicans to swing for the fences by pushing hard for legislative priorities that have a low chance of passing, Thune is more focused on holding his conference together and avoiding the disaster of another shutdown right before the midterm election.

A Republican senator close to Thune who requested anonymity to discuss tensions between Trump and the Senate GOP leadership said the top priority should be to keep the government funded through the Nov. 3 election.

“The most important thing we need to do between now and Oct. 1 is to make sure we don’t shut the government down,” the lawmaker said.

And the Thune-allied senator pushed back at the White House criticism directed at the Senate majority leader for the upper chamber’s failure to pass the SAVE America Act.

“The problem is not the Republican leadership. The problem is we can’t get the Democrats and the White House” to agree to a proposal to strengthen election security, the source said.

A second Republican senator who requested anonymity to talk about Leavitt’s criticism of Thune and Trump’s criticism of the Senate said that Thune is acting in the interests of his Senate colleagues by pushing back on some White House demands.

“Thune is representing his conference well,” the GOP senator said.

The lawmaker also noted that Trump has complained about the Senate’s failure to pass the SAVE America Act for months and has repeatedly urged Thune and other Republican senators to abolish the filibuster so they can pass election reform legislation with a simple majority vote.

Thune has repeatedly pushed back on that demand by declaring there aren’t nearly enough Republican votes to abolish the filibuster.

Tillis on Thursday applauded Thune’s decision to hold the House-passed budget resolution for use in September, if necessary, as a vehicle to pass a continuing resolution to keep the government open if Democrats try to block a stopgap funding bill — as they did in 2025, which led to a 43-day government shutdown.

“At the end of the day I think we’re wasting our time and we’re being dishonest to the American people to say that we need SAVE Act passed because there’s no way on God’s green earth it can be implemented by this election, so we have time to deal with that,” he said.

“I want to use this as a vehicle to fund the government. Schumer is sending all the signals he’s going to play shutdown politics,” Tillis added, referring to Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.).  

Thune, however, says he first wants to schedule a vote through the regular order on a continuing resolution to fund the government through Dec. 4 before trying to use the budget reconciliation to circumvent Democratic opposition to a funding stopgap.

“Let’s try the bipartisan route and see what happens,” the Senate majority leader said.

Thune is expected to schedule a vote on a clean government funding stopgap before the Senate adjourns for the five-week August recess.

data.title: Kagan says Supreme Court is not a 'rubber stamp' for Trump
data.description:
Supreme Court Justice Elena Kagan said Thursday that while the nation’s highest court has a conservative majority, it is not a “rubber stamp” for President Trump despite multiple rulings in his favor in major cases.

Speaking at a judicial conference hosted by the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals, Kagan said the court has maintained its independence by checking executive power in a number of cases.

“I don’t see this as a court that is just like, ‘We’re just going to rubber stamp what the current administration does.’ You know, quite the opposite,” Kagan said at the conference in Bellevue, Washington. “I think that that is a bad rap.”

The liberal justice pointed to decisions striking down Trump’s sweeping tariffs and rejecting his efforts to end birthright citizenship for children born in the U.S. to undocumented migrants.

“Is this court very conservative? Does it do a bunch of things that are out of the playbook of the conservative legal movement that I think are deeply wrong? You know, yes,” Kagan said. “But is this court the kind of puppet for the current administration? I think definitely not.”

She described the court’s invalidation of Trump’s tariffs as one of the most consequential rebukes of a sitting president.

“Tariffs are probably the key policy issue for this president. I mean, something he campaigned on, he ran on, he cared an enormous amount about,” she said. “I don’t think that there are all that many decisions in the recent times, where a court strikes down such an important policy to a sitting president.”

Kagan acknowledged disagreeing with many recent decisions, including rulings she said “eviscerated” the Voting Rights Act.

“You just have to deal with the fact that there’s a conservative court doing things that conservative jurists have long thought it appropriate to do, and I can really dislike that. But it has nothing to do, I think, with becoming the arm of this current administration,” she said.

The justice also reflected on Trump’s attendance at oral arguments over his birthright citizenship order earlier this year — the first time a sitting president attended Supreme Court arguments.

“He was just like a person who came in and sat down and then had to listen to an argument,” Kagan said, adding that many in the audience could not see him.

data.title: Federal judges allow Tennessee congressional map dividing majority-Black Memphis district
data.description:
A federal judicial panel on Thursday allowed Tennessee to use a new congressional map that divides a majority-Black district in Memphis, rejecting an effort to block the plan ahead of the state’s primary elections in August.

A three-judge panel declined to issue a preliminary injunction against the new map, clearing the way for the new congressional boundaries and a win for Republicans as the party seeks to protect its fragile majority in the House.

President Trump called on Republican-led states to redraw their maps following a landmark Supreme Court ruling weakening the Voting Rights Act and striking down Louisiana’s majority-Black congressional district as an unconstitutional racial gerrymander.

After the ruling, Tennessee revisited its congressional districts and sought to redraw its boundaries, prompting opponents to challenge a map approved by the state’s Republican-controlled legislature in May.

Opponents argued Republican lawmakers were motivated at least in part by racial discrimination when they dismantled the Memphis-area district, which they argue violated the Equal Protection Clause of the U.S. Constitution.

The panel of federal judges, which consisted of two Trump appointees and one Obama appointee, wrote in their opinion that plaintiffs likely would not win their lawsuit because they “lack any direct evidence of racial motivation.”

“The road to a reliably 9-0 map runs through Memphis,” the judges wrote in their opinion. “Thus, political motivations readily explain the map’s dilutive effects.”

Tennessee is among at least eight Republican-led states that have pursued congressional redistricting efforts ahead of the midterms as the GOP looks to preserve its majority in Congress. Democrats have condemned the efforts as attempts to weaken minority voting strength.

The Memphis-based district, which has a majority-Black population and has been represented by Rep. Steve Cohen (D-Tenn.), was broken up and placed into three separate districts stretching towards the suburbs of Nashville.

Following the Supreme Court ruling in May, the Congressional Black Caucus called on the nation’s largest corporations to oppose the redistricting efforts, arguing that “when voting rights are weakened and entire communities are systemically denied fair representation, the legitimacy and stability of the institutions that underpin our economy are weakened as well.”

data.title: USDA: Food supply is safe despite cyclospora, screwworm fights
data.description:
Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins sought to reassure Americans on Thursday that the nation’s food supply remains safe despite an ongoing Cyclospora outbreak and the spread of the New World screwworm.

“The food supply remains the best, the safest, the most affordable in the world, and will continue to do so,” Rollins said in a Thursday interview with NewsNation’s Blake Burman on “The Hill.”

The USDA chief said ensuring that the nation’s food supply is the agency’s number one priority, insisting that food in the country is currently safe despite the outbreaks.

Federal health officials have been responding to a multistate Cyclospora outbreak tied to lettuce imported from Mexico. Illnesses stemming from the outbreak are from a parasite spread through contaminated food and water.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has confirmed more than 4,000 nationwide cases of cyclosporiasis since May 1, making it the largest outbreak on record.

Rollins said the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), which oversees foodborne illness investigations, is working to determine how the contaminant entered the U.S. food supply to prevent future outbreaks.

“FDA is getting their arms around [this outbreak] to better understand what happened and how to make sure we’re protecting America,” she said.

Rollins also used the outbreak to argue that the U.S. should produce more of its own food and agriculture, rather than relying on foreign imports.

“I think it, again, goes to our previous conversation about national security and reshoring a lot of the food that we need in this country.”

She said progress has also been made with containing the flesh-eating New World screwworm that can infect humans and pets, which has returned decades after it was eradicated.

“We only have 12 active cases in Texas, and those numbers are declining,” Rollins said in the interview. 

The agency is increasing the production of sterile flies used to suppress screwworm populations, calling the effort a key part of USDA’s eradication strategy, according to Rollins.

USDA’s Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service credited using sterile flies as a proven method that worked to eradicate New World screwworm from the U.S. in 1966, as well as eliminate a smaller 2017 outbreak in the Florida Keys.

data.title: Trump ramps up threats of major operations in Iran
data.description:
Welcome to The Hill’s Defense & NatSec newsletter
{beacon}

Defense &
National Security

Defense &
National Security

 

The Big Story 

Trump ramps up threats of major operations in Iran 

President Trump told Axios he’s nearing a decision on whether to launch a “massive attack” on Iran as the U.S. expands its targets and deploys heavier firepower in its daily strikes on the country. 

Associated Press/Saul Loeb

“I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it,” Trump told Axios reporter Barak Ravid in a brief interview.  

 

The president told the outlet that Israel, which jointly launched the conflict’s initial strikes with the U.S. on Feb. 28, would join these new military operations “in two minutes if I ask them to.” However, he added that the U.S. does not “need anybody” for a potential next attack. 

 

Earlier on Thursday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio described Trump’s military stance toward Tehran as a “head for an eye.” 

 

The U.S. military is also moving more B-1 bombers, refueling jets and medical personnel into the region.

 

Iran accused the U.S. of being responsible to strikes on a border crossing with Iraq, which would mark a significant expansion of its geographic targets. 

 

Taken together, many observers viewed these developments as pointing toward a return to an all-out war on par with the U.S.-Israeli operations that wound down in April. 

 

“Escalation has been the likely basecase because 1) the current standoff isn’t sustainable, 2) the US window for action is not open-ended, 3) the route to de-escalation is unattractive and difficult, and 4) little scope of Iran backing down,” Gregory Brew, a senior analyst on Iran at the Eurasia Group, wrote on X. “Things are going to get worse, before they get better.”

 

But Trump faces major risks if he turns sharply toward escalation, with growing concerns about dwindling U.S. weapons stockpiles and rising energy costs months out from the November midterms. 

 

Sina Toossi, a senior non-resident fellow at the Center for International Policy, said he still assessed a return to diplomacy as the more likely outcome. 

 

“The political costs of a deal would be significant. He would face intense criticism from the hawkish foreign policy establishment and influential elements of his own coalition, particularly pro-Israel groups and donors,” he wrote on X.

 

“But those costs may ultimately be far lower than owning another endless, enormously costly war with no clear path to strategic success,” he added. “A deal he can always be spin as a victory. A quagmire he cannot.”

 

Welcome to The Hill’s Defense & National Security newsletter, I’m Colin Meyn filling in for Ellen Mitchell — your guide to the latest developments at the Pentagon, on Capitol Hill and beyond.

 

Did someone forward you this newsletter? Subscribe here.

It marked the first attack …

The U.S. Navy will no longer release service history information for sailors, ending the decades-long practice to address what officials describe as security threats and harassment concerns. The move comes amid greater press restrictions imposed by Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, which include a requirement for reporters to be escorted by authorized personnel in the Pentagon.  Under the new policy, the Navy will typically …

 Full Story

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt did not offer any new information regarding the civilian nuclear deal Thursday after President Trump made it conditional on Saudi Arabia normalizing relations with Israel. “The president is always the final dealmaker, as you know, and this is something that he has mentioned on numerous occasions going back,” Leavitt told reporters Thursday at a press briefing. “I can provide you …

 Full Story
 

Coast Guard should get a leader like other military branches, GOP senators say

(NewsNation) — A trio of Republican senators introduced a bill intending to create a leadership position for the U.S. Coast Guard. The Coast Guard Leadership Modernization Act would establish a secretary of the U.S. Coast Guard, mirroring a position across all other branches of the armed forces. According to the legislation’s text, the position would report to the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) …

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) bristled when asked Thursday about White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt’s statement that President … Read more

data.title: FDA panel votes to add peptides to permitted compounding list despite opposition from agency scientists
data.description:
The Food and Drug Administration’s (FDA) committee on compounded drugs on Thursday broke with agency scientists and voted in favor of adding peptides to the list of substances compounding pharmacies can make, despite concerns being raised over the lack of demonstrated benefits. 

The FDA’s Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) heard public comments and saw presentation by agency staffers concerning the peptides BPC-157, KPV, TB-500 and MOTS-C. These peptides are purported to help with ulcerative colitis, wound healing, obesity and osteoporosis. 

PCAC was tasked with recommending whether to add these peptides to the 503A Bulks list, the substances that 503A compounding pharmacies can make and sell to consumers. 

By 7 p.m. EST on Thursday, the committee had voted in favor of adding BPC-157, KPV, and TB-500 to the list, though the vote was split nearly evenly with 8 voting yes, 6 voting no and one member abstaining.  

At the time of writing, the committee was still hearing comments and presentations about MOTS-c. 

While opponents to adding these peptides to the list argued there wasn’t enough data to prove safety and efficacy, supporters essentially argued there was not enough to disprove these claims either. 

Prior to the meeting, FDA scientists concluded in briefing material to the committee that all the peptides being considered lacked the necessary evidence to conclude that they were safe effective for treating the conditions they were purported to help treat. 

Staffers reiterated this stance on Thursday. One staffer noted potential issues like there being no standardization for peptides, sharing that the FDA had encountered many substances all being marketed as “BPC-157” for example that had different active molecules. 

“Inconsistent naming conventions can contribute to the [bulk drug substance (BDS)] being not well characterized because there is no basis for a long-term understanding of the composition of the BDS,” the staffer stated in their presentation. “There is no basis for an expectation that the common name will always identify the same BDS, the same specific chemical form and structure.” 

Supporters of adding these peptides to the list argued that doing so would direct consumers away from the gray market, where many eager shoppers source supposed “research-grade” peptides from online vendors overseas. 

In explaining her “yes” vote, PCAC member Melissa Loseke said, ” just last week, I had a patient whose research grade whatever that they got from some place that they brought to me to fix was laced with MDMA and ecstasy. That’s not protecting the American people.” 

Asare Christian, one of several PCAC members who own clinics offering peptides, argued, “We are always taking risks with patients with all the interventions we do as physicians. And this provides an avenue, an alternative, to support the already existing available tools that we have to take care of patients.” 

Several other members of the committee, however, concurred with FDA staffers that there simply was not enough evidence to supporting making these peptides available through compounding pharmacies. 

In their first vote on BPC-157, PCAC members Elizabeth Rebello, Brian Serumaga and Josh Mailman were among those who said they believed there was not enough evidence demonstrating efficacy. 

Brian Lee, associate professor of medicine at the Keck School of Medicine of USC, warned that while the vote on Thursday would not be an FDA approval, consumers would still perceive it as an endorsement of the peptides. 

“I think that the data that we do have, the existing randomized data, is showing that BPC-157 may be no better than placebo,” said Lee. “And knowing that most important serious safety events like liver damage, where there is potential concern here, aren’t detected by self report. So, I think this endorsement can be potentially harmful, and it cannot in good conscience vote yes.”
